div.font:
Steps
(0) DNS: the consumer resolves a custom domain. Because an SN-E creates no automatic Private Hosted Zone, you author a CNAME (or Alias) record in a Route 53 private hosted zone in the Network account, mapping the custom domain to the SN-E generated DNS name; at scale this is automated via EventBridge + Step Functions + Route 53. External / on-premises resolvers reach that record by forwarding the query into AWS through a Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoint; the record returns the SN-E IP.
(1) The consumer connects to the SN-E IP over its existing path to AWS (Direct Connect, Site-to-Site VPN, VPC peering, Transit Gateway, or Cloud WAN). A single SN-E reaches all services and resources associated to its service network.
(2) The SN-E carries the connection privately over PrivateLink into the Service Network. The Service Network IAM auth policy is still evaluated, so cross-environment isolation holds for SN-E consumers exactly as for SN-A.
(3) The Service Network forwards the raw TCP flow to the Resource Gateway for the target Resource Configuration.
(4) The Resource Gateway forwards to the shared resource (an interface VPC endpoint, the egress proxy, or an application target). The response returns along the same path.

a.text: Text is not SVG - cannot display
